title: 06 - Neoplasia - Exam Prep Questions
description: Third-party generated single-choice exam-prep questions for Chapter 6. The set is aligned primarily to the Chapter 6 textbook content and shaped to reflect the style, structure, and difficulty distribution of the RCPA Basic Pathological Sciences mock exam format. Choices are keyed so answers remain stable if an application randomizes display order.

disclaimer.endorsement_notice: These questions are third-party generated study materials. They are not produced by, reviewed by, approved by, endorsed by, or affiliated with the textbook authors, Elsevier, the Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia, or any other authority, institution, publisher, or examining body.
disclaimer.answer_key_note: Answers are included as stable choice keys with brief source-grounded rationales for study and review purposes.

questions.question:
A malignant neoplasm is composed of cells showing adipocytic differentiation and invasive growth.

What is the MOST appropriate name for this tumor?

answer.reason: Malignant mesenchymal tumors are named as sarcomas according to the line of differentiation; a malignant adipocytic tumor is a liposarcoma.

questions.question:
A small nodule of histologically normal pancreatic tissue is found in the wall of the duodenum.

What term BEST describes this lesion?

answer.reason: A choristoma is a congenital heterotopic rest of otherwise normal tissue located at an abnormal site.

questions.question:
A cervical biopsy shows full-thickness epithelial atypia and loss of maturation, but the basement membrane remains intact.

What is the MOST accurate diagnosis?

answer.reason: Full-thickness dysplasia confined above an intact basement membrane is carcinoma in situ; invasion requires breach of the basement membrane.

questions.question: Which of the following is the MOST reliable indicator that a neoplasm is malignant rather than benign?

answer.reason: Metastasis is a defining property of malignant tumors and does not occur in benign neoplasms.

questions.question:
Breast cancer mortality is much higher in the United States and Europe than in Japan, but rates move toward the host country after migration.

What is the BEST interpretation of this observation?

choices.text: Cancer incidence is determined mainly by immutable racial genetics
Environmental exposures are major determinants of many common cancers
All breast cancers are caused by inherited BRCA mutations
Cancer registries systematically overcount cases in Western countries

answer.reason: Geographic variation and migration studies strongly support environmental exposure as a dominant determinant of many common cancers.

questions.question: Why does cancer incidence generally rise with advancing age?

choices.text: Aging eliminates all growth suppressor pathways
Most cancers are congenital and only become detectable late in life
Somatic mutations accumulate over time and immune competence declines
Older patients are universally exposed to oncogenic viruses
Cell division ceases completely in youth and resumes in old age

answer.reason: Increasing age raises cancer risk mainly through mutation accumulation and, secondarily, reduced immune competence.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a precursor lesion associated with increased carcinoma risk?

choices.text: Bronchial squamous metaplasia and dysplasia in a smoker
Simple scar tissue after wound healing
Normal intestinal crypt hyperplasia in childhood
Physiologic endometrial proliferation during the menstrual cycle

answer.reason: Bronchial squamous metaplasia and dysplasia in habitual smokers are classic precursor lesions for lung carcinoma.

questions.question: What distinguishes a driver mutation from a passenger mutation in a cancer genome?

choices.text: A driver mutation alters a cancer gene in a way that confers selective advantage
A driver mutation is always inherited in the germ line
A passenger mutation always prevents metastasis
A passenger mutation always occurs before the initiating mutation
A driver mutation affects only mitochondrial DNA

answer.reason: Driver mutations directly contribute to the malignant phenotype by conferring growth or survival advantage, unlike passenger mutations.

questions.question:
A patient with chronic myeloid leukemia is found to carry the Philadelphia chromosome.

What is the key molecular consequence of this abnormality?

choices.text: Constitutive activation of a fusion tyrosine kinase
Silencing of the APC gene by promoter methylation

answer.reason: The Philadelphia chromosome creates the BCR-ABL fusion gene, which encodes a constitutively active tyrosine kinase.

questions.question: In Burkitt lymphoma, translocation of MYC most directly produces which of the following?

choices.text: Constitutive overexpression of a growth-promoting transcription factor
Mismatch repair deficiency with microsatellite instability

answer.reason: MYC translocation places the gene under active immunoglobulin regulatory elements, causing sustained expression of a growth-promoting transcription factor.

questions.question:
A breast carcinoma shows HER2 amplification.

What is the most direct biologic consequence of this alteration?

choices.text: Constitutive or exaggerated growth factor receptor signaling

answer.reason: HER2 amplification increases expression of a growth factor receptor family member, making tumor cells highly responsive to proliferative signaling.

questions.question:
A RAS mutation blocks hydrolysis of bound GTP.

What is the immediate effect of this mutation on signaling?

choices.text: RAS remains locked in an active signal-transmitting state
RAS becomes unable to bind any guanine nucleotide
RAS is shuttled into lysosomes for degradation
RAS prevents transcription factor activation downstream

answer.reason: Mutated RAS cannot efficiently hydrolyze GTP to GDP, so it remains constitutively active and continues transmitting proliferative signals.

questions.question: What is the principal normal function of RB protein?

choices.text: Driving cells from G2 into mitosis
Maintaining the G1/S checkpoint by restraining E2F-dependent transcription
Mediating death receptor signaling through FAS
Repairing pyrimidine dimers caused by UV light

answer.reason: RB is the key governor of the G1/S checkpoint and restrains E2F until pro-growth signaling leads to RB phosphorylation and release of the brake.

questions.question: Loss of APC function most directly promotes neoplasia through which pathway?

choices.text: Accumulation of beta-catenin with activation of growth-promoting transcription
Enhanced repair of double-strand DNA breaks

answer.reason: APC normally helps degrade beta-catenin; loss of APC permits beta-catenin accumulation and inappropriate transcription of proliferative genes.

questions.question:
A cancer is intensely PET-positive despite adequate oxygen delivery.

What metabolic program BEST explains this finding?

choices.text: Exclusive reliance on fatty acid oxidation
High glucose uptake with aerobic glycolysis to generate biosynthetic intermediates

answer.reason: The Warburg effect features avid glucose uptake and aerobic glycolysis, which supports synthesis of cellular building blocks as well as energy production.

questions.question: Follicular lymphoma commonly survives because of overexpression of which anti-apoptotic protein?

answer.reason: Follicular lymphoma classically carries a t(14;18) that drives BCL2 overexpression and blocks intrinsic apoptosis.

questions.question: Which event most commonly allows cancer cells to achieve limitless replicative potential?

answer.reason: Most cancers avoid telomere-driven crisis and senescence by reactivating telomerase, thereby maintaining telomere length.

questions.question:
A solid tumor remains clinically occult until it acquires the capacity to grow beyond 2 mm in diameter.

Which new capability is MOST necessary for this transition?

answer.reason: Solid tumors generally cannot enlarge beyond 1 to 2 mm without inducing angiogenesis to supply oxygen and nutrients.

questions.question: What is the FIRST major step in the local invasion of a carcinoma?

choices.text: Attachment to novel extracellular matrix components
Degradation of the basement membrane by proteases
Loosening of tumor-cell contacts, often through reduced E-cadherin function

answer.reason: Invasion begins with reduced intercellular adhesion, classically via loss of E-cadherin-mediated cell-cell contact.

questions.question:
A tumor expresses high levels of PD-L1 on its surface.

What is the MOST likely effect of this alteration?

choices.text: Activation of cytotoxic T cells through costimulation
Enhanced assembly of MHC class I molecules
Inactivation of cytotoxic T cells through an immune checkpoint pathway

answer.reason: PD-L1 engages PD-1 on cytotoxic T cells and suppresses their anti-tumor activity as part of immune checkpoint-mediated evasion.

questions.question: Hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer syndrome is caused by inherited defects in which system?

answer.reason: HNPCC reflects inherited mismatch-repair defects, producing microsatellite instability and markedly increased colorectal cancer risk.

questions.question: Tumor-associated macrophages are thought to promote malignant progression primarily by doing which of the following?

choices.text: Creating a microenvironment that supports angiogenesis, fibroblast activation, and immune suppression
Repairing mismatched DNA bases in tumor cells
Preventing tumor cells from contacting extracellular matrix
Forcing tumor cells into terminal differentiation

answer.reason: Tumor-promoting inflammation supports growth, angiogenesis, invasion, and immune evasion; M2-type macrophages are strongly implicated in this supportive microenvironment.

questions.question: Which statement about chemical carcinogens is MOST accurate?

choices.text: Indirect-acting carcinogens do not require metabolic activation
Direct-acting carcinogens cannot be used therapeutically
Indirect-acting carcinogens become dangerous only after conversion to an ultimate carcinogen
Tumor promoters cause DNA mutation directly without affecting proliferation
Benzo[a]pyrene is an example of a direct-acting carcinogen

answer.reason: Indirect-acting carcinogens require endogenous metabolic conversion, often by enzymes such as cytochrome P-450, to become ultimate carcinogens.

questions.question: What is the characteristic DNA lesion produced by ultraviolet radiation?

answer.reason: UV light induces pyrimidine dimers in DNA; failure of nucleotide excision repair increases skin cancer risk.

questions.question: Which viral protein is central to the transforming activity of HTLV-1?

answer.reason: HTLV-1 Tax stimulates proliferation, enhances survival signaling, and promotes genomic instability in infected T cells.

questions.question: High-risk HPV promotes carcinogenesis chiefly because E6 and E7 neutralize which pair of tumor suppressors?

answer.reason: HPV E6 degrades p53 and increases TERT expression, while E7 binds RB and disables key CDK inhibitors, driving cell-cycle progression.

questions.question: What is the dominant mechanism by which chronic HBV and HCV infection promote hepatocellular carcinoma?

choices.text: Direct replacement of all host tumor suppressor genes
Immunologically mediated chronic injury with regeneration and reactive oxygen species generation
Exclusive activation of mismatch repair genes
Permanent inactivation of E-cadherin by promoter fusion
Universal insertion of viral DNA beside MYC

answer.reason: HBV and HCV are linked to hepatocellular carcinoma mainly through chronic inflammatory injury, regenerative proliferation, and ROS-mediated DNA damage, with additional signaling contributions from viral proteins.

questions.question:
A gastric MALT lymphoma regresses after antibiotic eradication therapy.

What underlying mechanism BEST explains this response?

choices.text: The lymphoma depends on H. pylori-driven immune stimulation for continued growth
The lymphoma cells require ultraviolet light-induced pyrimidine dimers
The tumor contains only direct-acting chemical carcinogens
The tumor is caused by constitutive HER2 amplification
The malignant cells cannot survive unless E-cadherin is overexpressed

answer.reason: H. pylori stimulates a polyclonal lymphoid response from which MALT lymphoma can emerge, and early tumors may remain dependent on that antigen-driven immune environment.

questions.question: Which laboratory technique is MOST useful for classifying leukemias and lymphomas on the basis of cell-surface phenotype?

answer.reason: Flow cytometry uses labeled antibodies to define cell-surface markers and is routinely used to classify leukemias and lymphomas.

questions.question: When planning treatment for a newly diagnosed solid cancer, which statement is MOST accurate?

choices.text: Histologic grade is consistently more clinically useful than stage
TNM staging assesses the primary tumor, regional nodes, and distant metastases
Stage ignores lymph node involvement because nodes are not prognostically relevant
Tumors are staged only after death or autopsy
Grading depends solely on tumor size

answer.reason: TNM staging describes the primary tumor, nodal spread, and distant metastases, and stage is generally more clinically useful than grade.
